title: Sussex pond pudding
subtitle: Whole lemon steamed inside a suet crust with butter and brown sugar.
excerpt: A whole lemon buried inside a suet pastry casing with butter and sugar, then steamed for three hours until the filling collapses into a sharp, buttery sauce that floods out when the pudding is cut open. One of the most extraordinary British puddings. Serves four to six.
sourceNotes: Sussex pond pudding. The recipe appears in British cookbooks from the 18th century, named for the pool of buttery sauce that spills from the pudding when sliced. It was popularised in the 20th century by food writer Jane Grigson in English Food (1974). The dish is documented in Mrs Beeton's later editions and in local Sussex culinary literature.

content.text: Sussex pond pudding is remarkable: a whole lemon steamed inside a suet crust for three hours until the rind softens completely and the filling becomes a flood of sharp, caramel-bitter sauce. The effect when the pudding is cut open is theatrical and delicious. Use an unwaxed lemon and prick it all over with a skewer so the juices escape into the filling.

items.prepNote: unwaxed, whole, pricked all over with a skewer

content.text: Line the basin and add the filling

Mix {{self-raising-flour}} of flour, {{lard}} of grated lard and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt together. Add {{whole-milk}} of milk gradually to form a soft dough. Roll two-thirds of the dough into a round large enough to line a greased 1.2-litre pudding basin. Line the basin, pressing the dough against the sides with no gaps. Put half the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter pieces and half the {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark sugar into the lined basin. Prick {{lemon}} lemon all over with a skewer and set it on top. Add the remaining butter and sugar around and over the lemon.

Roll the remaining dough into a lid and press firmly to seal the edges, dampening with water if needed. Cover with pleated baking paper and foil and tie securely. Set in a large saucepan with boiling water reaching halfway up the basin. Cover and steam over medium-low heat for 3 hours, topping up with boiling water every 30 minutes. Turn out onto a deep plate to catch the sauce.

items.symptom: Lemon rind still hard after steaming
items.fix: Three hours at a steady simmer is correct. Less time and the rind does not soften enough to eat.

items.cause: Gaps in the pastry lining or imperfect seal at the lid.
items.fix: Press the pastry firmly against the basin with no thin patches, and seal the lid thoroughly. A small leak is inconvenient but the pudding is still edible.

items.symptom: Lemon not pricked; rind bitter rather than giving
items.fix: Prick all over, deeply, so the juice is absorbed into the butter and sugar filling as it cooks.

content.text: Sussex pond pudding is one of the most unusual of British puddings: a whole lemon inside a suet crust is a startling combination on paper but an entirely logical one at the table. The sharpness of the lemon cuts through the sweetness of the brown sugar and the richness of the butter. The sauce is extraordinary. Few puddings reward the effort as immediately as this one does when it is cut open.
